name: Nerthus
description: Nerthus is a Germanic earth goddess mentioned by Tacitus in his Germania. She is associated with fertility, peace, and prosperity. Her name is believed to be related to the Norse god Njörðr, suggesting a possible Proto-Germanic origin for both deities. Tacitus describes a ritual where Nerthus is transported in a covered wagon, drawn by cows, and accompanied by peace and joy. During her travels, all weapons are laid aside, and peace reigns. Afterward, the wagon and her veil are cleansed in a secret lake by slaves, who are then drowned. This ritual highlights Nerthus's connection to the earth's bounty and the cyclical nature of life and death. Some scholars interpret Nerthus as a predecessor to later fertility goddesses in Germanic and Norse traditions. Her worship likely involved agricultural rites and celebrations of the earth's abundance. The secrecy surrounding her rituals emphasizes her sacred and powerful nature. Her role in maintaining peace and ensuring fertility underscores her importance to the Germanic tribes who revered her. The drowning of the slaves is a stark reminder of the sacrifices sometimes made to appease powerful deities.
subtitle: Earth Goddess of Peace and Fertility

keyMyths.title: Nerthus's Procession
keyMyths.description: Tacitus describes Nerthus traveling in a covered wagon, bringing peace to the land. Afterwards, the wagon and her veil are cleansed in a secret lake.

keyMyths.title: The Drowning of the Slaves
keyMyths.description: After the cleansing ritual, the slaves who attended to Nerthus are drowned in the lake. This act emphasizes the sacredness and danger of the goddess.

keyMyths.title: Weapons Laid Aside
keyMyths.description: During Nerthus's travels, all weapons are laid aside, demonstrating her power to bring peace. This underscores her association with tranquility and harmony.
